results.possibleIdentity: Kiril Sinkel is a retired electronic engineer and computer programmer who was active in New York City during the late 1960s and 1970s before transitioning into software development for digital imaging and photography. During his early career, he worked as an electronic engineer for Polarad Electronics Corp. in New York City, specializing in instrumentation and electronic engineering. He was likely a participant or member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit that bridged the gap between the technical and artistic communities. Sinkel later became a prominent figure in the field of digital image processing as a developer for Digital Light & Color, where he created the "Picture Window" and "Picture Window Pro" software. His work in this area included developing specialized geometry transformation techniques, such as a kaleidoscopic tessellation method for transforming photographs. In his retirement, Sinkel resides in Belmont, Massachusetts, where he is an active study group leader at the Osher Lifelong Learning Institute (OLLI) at Tufts University. He has spent over a decade auditing courses in genetics, microbiology, and neuroscience at Boston University and teaches OLLI courses on subjects ranging from the genomics revolution to the history of science.
